topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, stark white against the muted grey of my screen. A name I hadn't seen in fifteen years blinked back at me: Mark. My breath hitched, a sudden, tight knot forming in my chest. I hadn't interacted with this person since the cruel, echoing years of middle school, when his shadow loomed over everything. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, trembling slightly. I stared at the profile picture—a forced, wide grin that didn't reach his eyes. A cold, hard certainty settled in my stomach. I immediately minimized the message, pulling my headphones down over my ears as if the sound of the notification itself was a threat. I walked to the window, peering out at the busy street below, cataloging every passing face, every movement. Every instinct screamed at me to keep a wide berth, to be prepared for an ambush, even in this digital space.

[story 2]

"Who is this?" I muttered under my breath, the sound barely audible over the hum of the refrigerator. The request was undeniably from Mark. My jaw tightened, the muscles in my neck corded with tension. I didn't click it. Instead, I opened a dozen different browser tabs, searching his name, cross-referencing old school records, anything to gather data. Each search felt like an excavation, a careful sifting through debris, looking for traps. I kept glancing towards the front door, my posture stiff, shoulders pulled back as if bracing for impact. A nervous energy buzzed just beneath my skin, a restless thrumming that made my hands twitch. I paced the small living room three times before finally deciding to ignore the digital intrusion entirely, choosing instead to focus on the tangible reality of the room—the placement of the books, the way the light fell across the floor.

[story 3]

The chime sounded, jarringly loud in the quiet evening. It was a connection request, and the sender's name made my entire body lock up. I didn't immediately look at the details. Instead, I stood perfectly still, my gaze sweeping the periphery of my apartment. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy sensation on my palms. Every nerve ending felt acutely aware of the space around me, cataloging potential escape routes and weak points in my immediate vicinity. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic rhythm I was desperately trying to slow down through sheer force of will. I leaned against the counter, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, adopting a defensive stance, waiting for whatever came next with a palpable sense of readiness.
